speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you in 1933, when Hitler came to power in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, of course, still living in Chatteris. It was January when he took power, which he did lawfully by the Act of Enabling. And I was working on a farm for the Charles family and it was wintertime. And my job in the wintertime was looking after all the winter stock. Now, we had a lot-- we had a number of farms, but I worked on one which was called [INAUDIBLE] Farm. And there, we had a foreman who I worked under and who was virtually my boss. My father had died a couple of years before and things were very, very tight. And my main consideration at that time was ensuring I kept my job and my income to help my widowed mother. But that didn't stop us reading the papers. And of course, we got filtered news. But it was a hard life. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --the effect of the war, First World War, the casualties, and I remember men being brought home on stretchers after they'd been in hospital for a year or two. And if it meant that there was going to be a stable Germany, then I didn't think anybody thought much else about it. Because although we'd had all this bashing up of the Jews and that, we understood that. We didn't accept it. You know, it was-- to put it quite bluntly, it was bloody cruel. But the fact of the matter is we were much more concerned with our own problems. I was a boy of 15. And the first things I think which really registered with us was the Crystal Night, the burning of the books, and the burning down the Reichstag, which was obviously engineered, and that dreadful trial of the Dutchman. But that was the situation. We knew of it. We didn't now of Dachau and those places, but they were still to come. But I don't even recall Jimmy Rothschild talking about it, although I'm sure he did, but probably not when I was at the meeting. He didn't come up all that much. So there I was, I was working on a farm. I was earning about-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --well, this would be '33. I was only about a pound a week, just over a pound a week. But again, you can't relate it on present day costs.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your family react?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that is very difficult to answer. Above all in everybody's mind was the preservation of their own families. And if you're a widow, with-- I think my mother's widows' pension was 10 shillings, which, about $0.25, today's money-- and she was expected to keep a family on that for a week. I think their own problems overshadowed everything at that time, in January 1933. Because they would be all the heating bills and everything else. Life was very hard in the UK then. Although we are on the victorious side, things were pretty rough going.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you discuss with your friends about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sure we talked about it. I'm sure we talked about it. But I think we talked about it I think more from what you might call a pseudo military side. The British Army aided by the French army, such as it was, aided by the Americans, who came in very, very late, had, in fact, destroyed the German command system. And the last thing anybody wanted was another war because there was a War Memorial. Everybody knew everybody else and there had been this colossal waste of life. Over a million British men and women died in that war. And so I think it's fair to say that most people were preoccupied with their own problems. And I think that is the best answer I can give you. Nobody went around shouting, joy for Hitler, I can tell you that, any more than they did for Mussolini. They were always joking, saying the only thing Mussolini did, but he did it very well, he made the trains run on time in Italy. And-- but-- we-- nobody really wanted the Second World War, I'm sure that-- absolutely certain of it-- except Hitler. But I'm now talking with hindsight, as far as he is concerned.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you now at the time-- '33, '34, '35-- about Nazis?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's draw a line at '34 because my mode of life completely changed in '34. Up till '34, you read the news with interest, but your prime task was to stay in employment, to help you play your part in the family. Now, in 1934, I joined the regularly Army, as I've told you, with a view long-term of joining the police. Well, once you joined the regular Army and got into the military machine, it was a little bit different. The machine permitted you to make weekly payments to your mum and small children. But then as far as the machine went for the family, my mother used to send me my local paper but that never had anything other than local news in it-- Fishing Club results, football results, hockey results, cricket results, agricultural shows, that sort of thing. But inside the Army, there was already a feeling that things were not right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But of course, the total change of life going into the Army, you had to have all the basic training so that when they said turn left, you turned left and when they said, turn right, you turned right. But then, of course, we-- after the basic training, which was totally military inspired-- you went to your service unit. And I went to Edinburgh, the capital of Scotland, and I joined the Field Regiment of Gunners there. And mind you, I was only a young lad. I was just a young lad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you receive your training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the basic training, I went to Woolwich, which is the home of the Royal Regiment of Artillery and still is to this day and has been since I think 1716, when the regiment was first formed.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you enlist?

speaker: interviewee
text: In April of 1934. And I enlisted for six years. Unfortunately, in the fifth year, Hitler invaded Poland and the United Kingdom was at war with Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your mother tell you when you enlisted?

speaker: interviewee
text: She wasn't really happy about it, but I'd rather not talk about that. The-- she was a mother and a priceless one. She didn't think very much about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm not sure what you'd be frightened. Fear of what?

speaker: interviewer
text: When you enlisted, the prospects of what could happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I don't think you bothered about that at that age. I'm sure we didn't. I didn't, anyway, I'm sure of that. The-- you just-- you volunteered, you see. And everybody with you was a volunteer. We had no conscription or anything like that in 1934. We had a very close liaison with the fellow countries like Canada, Australia, New Zealand, South Africa, Rhodesia, all members of the British Commonwealth-- and incidentally, all equal members. We had very closely liaison with them. But we had never liaison at all with the French, who had been our allies. The United States had gone into isolation and gone dry. And so really, we-- we were content. We were members of the Empire on which the sun never set and we all worked together and we all pulled together. And we used to have the Australian cricket team used to come to England and play cricket with us and they used to thrash us sometimes and sometimes we thrashed them. But it was all great fun and it was like going home. Wherever you went, you were going home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you when you enlisted?

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jews in your basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if there were, they kept it to themselves. I don't remember any. I'm sure I would have remembered.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe the day of the training.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Well, in those days we had horses, and horses pulled the guns and did everything else. And the day started with horses-- feeding them, grooming them, washing them, watering them. All took time. And when you'd done all that, then you could go and have your breakfast. And if you were slow eating your breakfast, then you were in the [INAUDIBLE] because you had to get on parade. And you never stopped, never stopped. There was no windbreaks between sessions on the square or studying or in the gymnasium or whatever it was. It was pretty hard going. But you were all volunteers. You'd all said, we'll do it and you were equally determined one way or another that you wouldn't let your mate down and your mate wouldn't let you down. And that was it. And then after whatever it was, 14 weeks, you qualified. Every Sunday you had to go to church. And the-- that was a great day for the Church of England, too, because we all used to have to go on parade. But I would put myself down as an oddity, a chapel man. So I used to go to chapel on my own. I used to go with a Welshman, and he was a chapel man. And we used to go to the local chapel. We were allowed to walk there on her own. And then when we'd finished, we used to have to get a hold of the minister and he would sign the ticket for us to prove we'd been. If he didn't, then we got in hot water.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you finish the basic training?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it would be July of '34. And then we caught the train and we went up to Edinburgh to join our regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your grade?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the very lowest gunner-- gunner. You see, it's a private in the infantry, trooper the Calvary, trooper the Armored Corps, gunner in the Artillery.

speaker: interviewer
text: What does a gunner do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it's not a facetious answer, Christian, but he does as he's told. And if he doesn't, he's in the shit. But you got opportunities to get on, you know. Once you got into a service unit, you had an opportunity to train for various things like-- which required high grade maths and that sort of thing. You did signaling, we learned to-- most of our equipment was World War I signaling equipment then. And you learned to ride and do various things. But the thing that-- again, it's with afterthought-- when we were in Edinburgh, we never worked with the infantry. Now, that's significant because we were field gunners but nobody ever thought of [? mixing ?] the two together and training together, inter-service training. We never worked with the Navy, we never worked with Royal Air Force. In fact, the only time we ever got out of barracks was to do some individual training ourselves or to go to the castle and fire a salute when some dignitary or some warship came into the Firth or Forth. But nobody ever thought-- this was 1934-- nobody ever thought of exercising with infantry, what we call all arms training. And that was five years before the war. So we weren't very-- we weren't very militaristic. And nobody seemed to really mind. It wasn't until we actually left Edinburgh and came down to Aldershot Command that we met a different world, where clearly-- this was 1936 and the things were happening on the continent and in Germany where people who could think, they had realized that war was in the offing. And then we started working with the infantry and everybody else. And we weren't very good. But we got better.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your service number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. It doesn't really mean very much but, it was 835724. But I don't know-- I only kept that as long as I was in the ranks. When I was commissioned, they gave me another number.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the unit name and number?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was-- I joined in Edinburgh a regiment which was number 16. It was a regular field regiment. And I stayed with them and went to France with them in September 1939. And then I came home from France and I was put into an officer's training school. And then I rejoined that regiment when it came back from Dunkirk and I was with them only for a very short time and then I went off as a reinforcement to a regiment which had no training at all. And then eventually, I went to Special Forces, which was the Mountain Artillery. And that will bring us up to 1941-42.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just coming back before the war in '38, where were you in the Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: In?

speaker: interviewer
text: During the Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: Kristanna?

speaker: interviewer
text: In 1938, November.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in 1938 November, Munich had just taken place in 1938. That would be September, I think, Munich was. And I was near Aldershot with my regiment and we, in fact, had just been re-equipped with guns and we'd just lost our horses and we'd just been re-equipped with mechanical transport. So we were new boys all the way around.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the burning of the books and the shattering of the glass?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, that's when-- that-- we would have been near Aldershot, a little village called Ewshot. But it was all in the Aldershot command.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was your reaction to that event?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think people were very angry about it. It-- it-- it made it quite clear that war was very likely. War was very likely. There was nothing we could do. There was nothing we could do about it because we were just ordinary soldiers. And as I said to your earlier, what did you did? You did as you were told. But-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --I think it be a good idea to mention Munich because the Munich Agreement, Hitler thought he got everybody on toast, and-- but he didn't cater with the British attitude. I think the British attitude was after Munich, Chamberlain had done his best to preserve peace. Some people weren't very pleased with that because they felt that he had acted in a manner which he shouldn't have done. But they're entitled to do that in a democracy. And I know that in Munich, at the time of Munich, we were in no fit state to go to war. As I've just told you, we'd just been getting new guns and we'd just been converted from animal draft to mechanical draft. In actual fact, we'd just received our first wireless set-- field wireless sets. And we were in no fit state whatsoever to go to war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I can only say through my own eyes, we would have fought anything. But I knew that we-- we weren't fit to go to war. We-- I mean, I just know that to be true. And we thought that we got grace. Well, we did get grace. We got grace for a year. And when people say, why didn't the British do more, I don't see that they could have done anymore because no money had been spent on the Army. That is why we were still on two shillings a day. There were only volunteers, there were no-- there was no conscription. In fact, conscription didn't come in until we had the militia scheme in I think it was May 1939. And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] --everybody hoped and prayed that it would-- it wouldn't go any farther. But of course, we now know that he never intended stopping. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Although Great Britain was one of the great powers, she had virtually divested herself of her armed force. The Navy was there, but the Air Force had to be totally re-equipped. And in fact, I remember seeing the first Hurricanes flying. So no, I think that at that time, they-- there was no alternative to it. Because the French were no good. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course, the French were the major military power. But they weren't any good anymore than they were in 1940. They went into the bag in the millions in 1940 instead of 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go when the war broke out?

speaker: interviewee
text: The war broke out on the 3rd of September '39 and I was in France a week later with my regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where to?

speaker: interviewee
text: We sailed to Saint-Nazaire on the west coast on the Loire River, the mouth of the River Loire. In fact, we landed where three or four years later, there was a big kommando raid, the greatest raid of all, at Saint-Nazaire, when they drove HMS into the dock gates, the object being to close that port to the German [INAUDIBLE] ships. And then with my division, we went up and took up our positions on the Belgian frontier.

speaker: interviewer
text: What division was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Second Infantry Division.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the unit?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16Th Field. And we-- we were a good regiment. By now, by working day and night and we had a new CO who drove us along. We were an efficient regiment, a very efficient regiment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your rank?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was now Sergeant Major. I had got up-- I was what they call a Warrant Officer, Class III, and my job was with the guns. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my corps commander was Sir John Dill, who later became Chief of the Imperial General Staff and then he went to Washington as the British representative of the Combined Chiefs of Staff Committee. And he did a great job with the Americans, before the Americans came in. And then they learned enough about him to trust him. In fact, he's buried in Arlington Cemetery in the United States, one of the very few foreigners buried in that-- and they built an equestrian statue. The Americans put it on his grave. I have a figure of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the end of '39, how long did you think the war would last?

speaker: interviewee
text: It certainly didn't go how I thought it would. The-- there was great secrecy. We didn't know what the plans were, we ordinary soldiers. We knew what our tasks were, but we didn't know what the plans were. And the advance to the River Dijle position-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --well, I mean, we didn't know it. But I was at the Officers' School in the United Kingdom at the time and we watched with trepidation at the clear defeat that was going on. And-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] --when the order was given to evacuate the British Expeditionary Force and the soldiers started coming back, they were landed at ports in Kent-- Margate, Ramsgate, Dover-- and then they was put on the trains and they were sent up to the Aldershot area, where they were put into camps. And of course, the first troops evacuated were from the lines of communications. And I must say, they didn't create a very good cut. And they took all of us youngsters out of the Officers' Training School to remand the reception committees for these chaps coming back. Remember, over 300,000 were brought back and over 100,000 Frenchmen. They all went through our hands and we registered them. And I remember I was sitting at a table on Tweseldown racecourse, which is near Aldershot, and I was listening to all these chaps coming back. And I remember a chap coming up to my table-- and you didn't even look up to look at his face, it was just mechanical, get it done. The quicker you could get it done, there was another 1,000 waiting to be registered.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this chap said-- I said, number? And he gave me his number. And I said, name? And he said, Signaler [INAUDIBLE]. He was one of my signalers. I'd never been more pleased to see anybody. Oh, it was wonderful to see him. And anyway, he gave me an update of what was happening on the other side of the Channel. And then I was very friendly with a chap in the Officer Training School and he and I some days later was walking down to a hut.

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been up to the dining room to get our breakfast. And there coming up the road was my old battery commander. It was wonderful see him. I absolutely worshipped this chap-- great soldier, brave, honest, kind. And he could tear you off a strip if he had to. But a terrible time to live through. And then, of course, the regiments went off to their various places to report. But we actually registered them when they actually came back from Dunkirk itself to the port. They were put on these trains and just herded like a lot of sheep. And-- but when the fighting troops came back, it made you proud to be British, it really did. They hadn't been beaten.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there any Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we wouldn't know such. You know, we wouldn't know as such. They-- they-- we merely took their number, rank, and name and regiment and pushed him up to-- that regiment's over in that field, that regiment's over in that field. But we never had them as-- and you must remember, there was no Israel then, as I told you before. There were Polish Jews, French Jews, German Jews, Dutch Jews, British Jews, but there were no Jews, as such, with a nation of there own. And therefore, if he was wearing Polish badges, he was a Pole; if he was wearing French badges, well, he was a frog; and if he was wearing Belgian, he was Belgian. But there were none as such-- they weren't categorized. I think that's rather nice, really. They weren't treated differently. How that went on for [? Kosher ?] I don't know, but--
